questions.question: Define epidemiology. What are its uses in dentistry?
questions.answer:
Epidemiology: study of distribution and determinants of health-related states/events in populations, and application to control health problems.

Uses in dentistry: measure disease burden (DMFT, CPI); identify risk factors; plan services; evaluate programmes; screen populations; policy and resource allocation.
questions.analogy: Like a health detective — who has the disease, where, why, and what we can do about it at the population level.

questions.question: What is DMFT index? How do you calculate it?
questions.answer: DMFT = Decayed, Missing, Filled permanent Teeth. For individuals: count D (carious), M (missing due to caries), F (filled); sum = DMFT. For population: mean DMFT = sum of all individuals' DMFT / number of individuals. Only permanent teeth (28 or 32). Used for caries experience in epidemiology.
questions.analogy: Like a scorecard for tooth damage — D = bad, M = gone, F = fixed; the total tells you how much decay the person has had.

questions.question: Write a short note on STEPS IN DENTAL PUBLIC HEALTH.
questions.answer:
1. SURVEY
- Surveys are methods for collection of data, analysing and evaluating
them in order to determine the amount of disease problems. in a comm-
unity and also to identify cases that have not been identified.
- Various parameters assessed in a survey include -
a> socio economic status of the community
b> nature of distribution of the community
c> health problems of the community
d> resources available for eliminating the problem
e> attitudes of the community towards oral health
- The focus of attention in a survey is the population rather than an
individual
2. ANALYSIS
Information collected through a survey is subjected: to an analysis
in order to define the characteristics of specific health problems in
the community.
- Since in dental public health, the dentist is interacting with groups
of people, it is necessary to organise the data to make the analysis
easier
- The science of statistics is applied to arrive at the common descriptive
constants Like the mean, median mode and standard deviation in
order to achieve a correct diagnosis or to arrive at an accurate
analysis.

questions.question: Write a short note on CPITN PROBE.
questions.answer:
CPITN stands for Community Periodontal Index of Treatment
Needs.
Also called as WHO Periodontal Examination Probe and was first
described by WHO (TRS 621-1978)
This probe was designed for 2 purposes, namely measurement
of pocket depth and detection of subgingival calculus
The CPITN probe is both thin in the handle & is of very Light
weight (5 gins)
This probe is particularly designed for gentle manipulation of
the sensitive soft tissues around the teeth

questions.question: Write a short note on STRUCTURAL DETAILS.
questions.answer:
The pocket depth is measured through color coding with a
black mark starting at 3.5 mm & ending at 5.5 mm.
a
The probe has a ball tip of 0.5 mm diameter that allows
NG
easy detection of subgingival calculus. This feature combined
with the Light probe weight facilitates the identification of the
base of the pocket thus decreasing the tendency for false read-
ing by over measurement.
A variant of this basic probe has 2 additional lines at 8.5 mm
& 11.5 mm from the working tip. They are useful far performing
a detailed assessment & recording of deep pockets for making
treatment plans for complex periodontal therapy.
The joint working committee of WHO/FDI have advised the
manufacturers of CPITN probes to identify the instruments
as CPITN-E for the epidemiological probe with 3.5 mm &
5.5 mm markings, or CPITN -C for the clinical probe with
additional 8.5 mm & 11.5 mm markings.

questions.question: Write a short note on SCHOOL DENTAL NURSE.
questions.answer:
This is an operating auxiliary, who is permitted to diagnase dental
disease & to plan and carry out certain specified preventive and
treatment measure, including some operative procedures in the
treatment of dental caries and periodontal disease in defined groups
of people, usually school children.
T.A. Hunter, founder of the New Zealand Dental Association influen-
ced the formation of Dental Nurse Scheme
The Dental Nurse Scheme was established in Wellington, New
Zealand in 1921 due to the extensive dental diseases found in army
recruits during World War 1.
The training extends to over a period of 2 years to cover both the
reversible & Prreversible procedures.
Upon completion of course training, each school dental nurse is
assigned to a school where she is employed by the government
to provide regular dental care for 450-700 children
In New Zealand, the dental nurses are predominantly in the school-
based salaried service & are expected to provide care to the children
at nearby 6-month intervals.

c> to study the carrelation or association between two or more
attributes in the same population

studies

martality in the community
f> to evaluate the achievements of public health program

psychology.

dependent upon the group behaviour of the individuals, determined
by their culture.

program, social scientists are called in to access the process and
find & help rectify the drawbacks.

patterns.

community hygiene includes the care of the surroundings

Iatrogenic diseases is the name given to doctor-induced illness.

treatment plan.
Patients should not be bystanders in their treatment but active
participants.
Dentists sometimes attempt to direct a patient towards a
particular mode of treatment by stressing certain advantages

It is a breach of ethics (unethical) to mislead or misinform

ii> INFORMED CONSENT
Informed consent an essential component of a patients right
to autonomy is a two-step process
First information is presented to the patient by the doctor.
Secondly the patient satisfies himself / herself that he or
she understands, and based upon this understanding either
agrees or refuses to undergo the treatment.
Informed consent is the first stated and largest principle

questions.question: Write a short note on NATIONAL ORAL HEALTH POLICY.
questions.answer:
The National Oral Health Policy has been formulated by DCI, through
the inputs of 2 national workshops organised in 1991 and 1994 at
Delhi and Mysare respectively.
These workshops considered the recommendations of national
workshops on oral health goals for India, Bombay 1984 and a
draft oral health policy prepared by IDA in 1986.
The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, GOI accepted in
principle the National Oral Health Policy in 1995 to be included
in the National Health Policy.
10 POINT RESOLUTION
1> There is an urgent need far an Oral Health Policy far the
nation as an integral part of the National Health Policy
NG
2> Special well coordinated National Oral Health Program be
Launched to provide oral health care, both in the rural as
well as in the urban areas due to deteriorating oral health
conditions in the country as revealed by various epidemio-
logical studies.
Dentist /population ratio in the rural areas is 1: 3,00,000,
whereas 80% children and 60% adults suffer from caries.
3> A past of full time dental Advisor at appropriate level in
the Directorate General of Health Services (Dte. G.H.S)
should be created as a first step towards strengthening
the Technical wing of Dte. G.H.S
4> Studies have revealed that dental diseases have been increasing both
in prevalence and severity over the Last few decades.
There is therefore an urgent need to prevent the rising trend of
dental diseases in India.
5> The Council, therefore resolves that preventive and promotive
oral health services be introduced from the village Level onwards
& a pilot project on oral health care be launched by MOHFW
during 1995-96 in 5 districts; 1 each in 5 states.
6> The Council further resolves that legislative measures be adapted
to ensure a statuatory warning on the wrappers & advertisement
of sweets, chocolates & other retentive sugar 0 eatables

questions.question: Write a short note on CHANGING CONCEPTS OF PUBLIC HEALTH.

A> DISEASE CONTROL PHASE
- 1880 - 1920
- This phase aimed at the control of man's physical environment
eg - water supply sewage control
- These measures were not aimed at the control of any specific disease
- However, these measures vastly improved the health of the people
due to disease and death control.
B> HEALTH PROMOTIONAL PHASE
- 1920 1960
- This phase aimed at health promotion of individuals in addition to
disease control activities.
- It was initiated as personal health services such as mother and
child health services, school health services industrial health
services mental health and rehabilitation services.
- 2 important movements that were initiated during the early 20th
century were- -
a> Provision of basic health services through primary health
centres and sub centres for the rural and urban areas.
The concept of health centre was first quoted in 1920,
by Lard Dawson of England.
In 1931, the League of Nations Health Organisation called
for the establishment of health centres
b> The second greatme movement was the community develop-
ment program to promote village development through the
active participation of the whole community.
Although this program failed to survive establishment of
Primary Health Centres and Sub Centres provided the 0881 much
needed infrastructure for health services, specially in rural
areas.
c> SOCIAL ENGINEERING PHASE
- 1960 - 1980
- with advances in preventive medicine and practice of public health
the pattern of disease began to change in the developed world
- Many of the acute illness problems were solved, new health
problems in the form of chronic disease began to emerge,
eg - cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, alchoholism.
- These problems could not be tackled by the traditional approa-
ches to public health such as isolation, immunization and
G
disinfection nor could these be explained on the basis of the
Germ theory of disease.

engineering phase where...

questions.question: Write a short note on ASKOV DENTAL DEMONSTRATION.
questions.answer:
Askov is a small farming community with a population mostly of
Danish extraction.
It showed very high dental caries in the initial surveys made in
1943 and 1946.
During the period from 1949 to 1957, the section on Dental Health
of the Minnesota Department of Health supervised a demonstration
school dental health program in ASKOV, including caries prevention
and control, dental health education and dental care.
ALL recognised methods for preventing dental caries were used
in the demonstration with the exception of community water
fluoridation since until 1955 Askov had no community water
supply.
Dental care was rendered by a group of 5 dentists from nearby
communities employed by the Minnesota Department of
Health. These dentists also gave topical fluoride treatments.
Findings available through a 10 year period revealed
- 28% reduction in dental caries in deciduous teeth of
children aged 3-5 years
- 34% reduction in caries in permanent teeth of children
6-12 years old
- 14% reduction in permanent teeth of children
13-17 years old
- improvements in filled-tooth ratios.
Jo The program also had many intangible benefits such as vala good
health and dietary habits for the children to carry on to
adult life.

questions.question: Write a short note on WATER PURIFICATION ON LARGE SCALE.
questions.answer:
The components of a water purification system comprise of
A> Starage
B> Filtration
c> Chlorination
A> STORAGE
During starage, considerable amount of purification takes place by
1> Physical action - About 90% of the suspended impurities settle
down in 24 hours by gravity. The water becomes clearer allowing
penetration of Light.
2> Chemical action - The aerobic bacteria oxidise the organic
matter present in the water with the help of dissolved oxygen
reducing the content of free ammonia and increasing the conc-
of nitrates.
3> Biological action - During starage the pathogenic organisms
gradually die out. However, if the water is stared for Long periods,
there is Likelihood of development of vegetable growths such as
algae, which imparts bad smell and color to the water.
B> FILTRATION
Filtration is the second stage in the purification of water
The two types of filters used are -
1> Biological or Slow sand filter
2> Mechanical or rapid sand filter

questions.question: Write a short note on Elements of Slow sand Filter.
questions.answer:
1> Supernatant water - The depth of the supernatant water above
the sand bed varies from 1 to 1.5.m.
It provides a constant head of water so as to overcome the resistan
-ce of the filter bed.
It provides waiting period of some hours for the raw water to
undergo purification by sedimentation, oxidation and particle
agglomeration.
2> A bed of Graded Sand - The thickness of the sand bed is about
1 m. The sand grains have a diameter between 0.2-0.3. mm.
The sand bed is supported by a layer of graded gravel 30-40 cm
deep which also prevents the fine grains being carried into drainage
pipes.
Water percolates through the sand bed very slowly and it is subjec-
ted to mechanical straining, sedimentation, , absorption, oxidation
and bacterial action.
The rate of filtration lies between 0.1-0.4m³/hour/m² of sand
bed surface.
a
Vital Payer - slimy growth covering the surface of sand
bed is called as Schmutzdecke or vital Layer.
This Layer consists of threadlike algae, plankton, diatoms
and bacteria.
It takes several days for the vital layer to form fully &
when fully formed it extends for 2-3 cm into the top
partion of sand bed.
The formation of vital layer is called ripening of the
filter.
The vital layer is the heart of the slow sand filter. It
removes organic matter, holds back bacteria & oxidises
ammoniacal nitrogen into nitrates
3> An under drainage system 1 1 At the bottom of the filter bed is
the under drainage system. It consists of perforated pipes which
provides an outlet for filtered water 1 and also supports the filter
medium above.
4> A system of Filter control valves - They maintain a constant rate
of filtration. The venturimeter is used to measure the bed resistance
or lass of head.
When the resistance builds up the operator opens the regulating valve
so as to maintain a steady rate of filtration.
